mnemonics.title: PRIM Puts Roots In MST
mnemonics.content: PRIM's algorithm: Start from a vertex, grow MST like a tree. At each step, pick the MINIMUM weight edge connecting the tree to a new vertex. P-R-I-M = Pick, Record, Insert, Modify - pick minimum edge, record distances, insert into PQ, modify if better path found!

mnemonics.title: KRUSKAL Keeps Raising Swords
mnemonics.content: KRUSKAL's algorithm: Sort all edges by weight, then add edges one by one using Union-Find. KRUSKAL = Keep Repeatedly Using Sorted edges, Skipping edges that cause cycles. Remember: 'Sort and Union-Find' - sort edges, then use DSU to avoid cycles!

mnemonics.title: DIJKSTRA Doesn't Invite Negative Friends
mnemonics.content: DIJKSTRA's algorithm: Use min-heap to always process vertex with smallest distance. Works ONLY with non-negative weights. DIJKSTRA = Distances Initialized, Just Keep Searching To Find Right Answer. Remember: 'Positive weights only, Negative fails!'

mnemonics.title: BELLMAN Brings Relaxation N-1 Times
mnemonics.content: BELLMAN-FORD algorithm: Relax all edges N-1 times. Can handle negative weights and detect negative weight cycles. BELLMAN = Bring Extra Ledges, Let Many Edges Receive New Adjustments. Remember: 'Relax N times, then check for negative cycles!'

mnemonics.title: Cut Property: Cheapest Bridge Wins
mnemonics.content: Cut Property: In any cut of the graph, the minimum weight edge crossing the cut belongs to ALL MSTs. Think of it as the cheapest bridge between two islands - it MUST be built in any optimal solution!

mnemonics.title: Floyd-Warshall: Fly With Three Loops
mnemonics.content: Floyd-Warshall algorithm: Dynamic programming approach for all-pairs shortest path. Uses three nested loops. FLOYD = For Loop Reach Outer Loop Destination. O(V³) complexity but handles negative weights!

acronyms.usage: A spanning tree with minimum total edge weight that connects all vertices without cycles. Used in network design, clustering.
Finding shortest paths from one source vertex to all other vertices. Algorithms: Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford, BFS (unweighted).
Also called Union-Find. Used in Kruskal's algorithm to detect cycles and merge sets efficiently. Operations: Find, Union.
For unweighted graphs, BFS finds shortest path because it explores level by level. Uses Queue data structure.
Finding shortest paths between all vertex pairs. Algorithms: Floyd-Warshall, repeated Dijkstra/Bellman-Ford.
The tree formed by shortest paths from source to all vertices. Generated by Dijkstra or Bellman-Ford algorithms.
Operation: if dist[u] + w(u,v) < dist[v], then update dist[v]. Core operation in shortest path algorithms.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a greedy person at a buffet always picking the cheapest dish first. Visualize making local optimal choices - each step picks the minimum weight edge, building toward global optimum like assembling a puzzle piece by piece.

Visualize the graph as a piece of paper cut into two halves with scissors. The minimum weight edge crossing this 'cut' (bridge between halves) is ALWAYS in the MST. Imagine highlighting the cheapest bridge crossing the divide.
Draw a cycle in the graph. Visualize the heaviest edge in the cycle as a thick red line - it can be removed to get a lighter spanning tree. Think: 'Maximum edge in cycle is expendable!'

Imagine dropping a stone in a pond - ripples spread outward. The algorithm expands like water ripples from the source vertex, always reaching the nearest unvisited vertex first. Use min-heap visualization as a mountain where smallest distance is at the peak.

Imagine coins (edges) piled by weight. Pick coins one by one from smallest pile. Use Union-Find as separate buckets - if two vertices are already in the same bucket, skip that edge (would create cycle)! Visualize forest growing, merging trees.

Think of MST as a growing vine or tree branch. Start with one vertex (seed). At each step, visualize the tree reaching out with its cheapest branch to grab a new vertex. Like tentacles expanding, always grabbing the closest prey.

Imagine waves propagating through the graph. Each of N-1 passes relaxes all edges. Visualize distance values 'warming up' - initially infinity, gradually cooling down to optimal values like annealing metal.

Imagine going in circles and losing money each round - you'd keep getting richer by going around forever! Visualize a cycle with negative total weight as a 'money-making trap' that Bellman-Ford detects.

analogies.analogy: Building a railway network connecting all cities with minimum total track length. You want all cities connected (spanning) with minimum total cost (minimum). Like connecting villages with the least amount of rail tracks.

Like laying telephone wires between buildings across a city. Sort all possible connections by cost, then connect buildings one by one, making sure you don't create closed loops in the network. If two buildings are already connected, skip that wire!

Like planting a tree and watching it grow branch by branch. Start from the trunk (source vertex), each day add the cheapest branch that extends the tree to a new area. The tree grows organically from one point.

Like a GPS navigation system finding the shortest route to your destination. It always considers the nearest checkpoint first, updates routes when it finds shorter paths. Like asking locals for the shortest way, always choosing the shortest suggested route.

Like finding arbitrage opportunities in currency exchange where going through multiple exchanges might give you more money than starting with. If you can keep gaining money by cycling through exchanges (negative cycle), Bellman-Ford will detect it!

Like deciding what to buy each day with minimum money spent, hoping it leads to overall savings. At each step, pick the cheapest available option (local optimum), hoping it leads to minimum total spending (global optimum). But beware: sometimes locally optimal choices don't give global optimum!

Like merging friend groups. Initially, everyone is in their own group. When you connect two vertices, you merge their groups. If two people are already in the same group, adding another connection would create a 'circle' of friends (cycle)!

analogies.concept: Priority Queue in Dijkstra - Hospital Triage
analogies.analogy: Like a hospital emergency room where patients with the most critical (minimum distance/severity) condition are treated first. The priority queue ensures the most urgent (closest) vertex gets processed immediately, like triage in emergency.
